Facts.text: Saint Dionysius, Roman Pope (259-260), dies
Leo III is elected to serve as Pope on the day his predecessor was buried and is consecrated the following day
Wulfstan, Anglo-Saxon Archbishop of York (931-52) who supported Viking leaders, dies under arrest
Bishop of Paris Eudes de Sully condemns the Feast of Fools - the medieval festival where higher clergy changed places with lower clergy
Galeazzo Maria Sforza, Duke and ruler of Milan is assassinated by three court officials in Santo Stefano church on the feast day of St Stephens
Battle at Westbroek: Dutch army beats Utrecht
Long Parliament reforms in Westminster
Fateh Singh (6) and Zorawar Singh (7), sons of Guru Gobind Singh, are murdered by Wazir Khan for refusing to convert to Islam; they are now among the most hallowed martyrs in Sikhism
France & Austria sign treaty about Southern Netherlands
Expulsion of tea ships from Philadelphia
Battle of Geisberg: French defeat a force of Austrians and Prussians
George Washingtonis eulogized by Colonel Henry Lee as "1st in war, 1st in peace & 1st in hearts of his countrymen"
France & Austria signs Peace of Pressburg
Pennsylvania Academy of Fine Arts established, Philadelphia
British invasionary troop leaves Vlissingen
A theater fire in Richmond, Virginia kills the Governor of Virginia George William Smith and the president of the First National Bank of Virginia Abraham B. Venable.
Zamose, Modlin, Torgau surrenders to allied armies
Gaetano Donizetti's opera "Anna Bolena" premieres in Milan
Vincenzo Bellini's opera "Norma" premieres in Milan
1st gold seekers arrive in Panama en route to San Francisco
The Phi Delta Theta fraternity is founded at Miami University, Oxford, Ohio.

Treaty of Medicine Creek signed by Nisqually, Puyallup and Coast Salish peoples with Isaac Ingalls Stevens, Governor of Washington Territory, giving up 2.5 million acres to preserve fishing and gathering rights
Wood-pulp paper 1st exhibited, Buffalo
First ever inter-club football match between Hallam F.C. and Sheffield F.C. at Sandygate Road ground in Sheffield, England
Major Robert Anderson, under cover of darkness, concentrates his small force at Ft Sumter
Largest mass execution in US history: 38 Dakota men were executed via hanging in the aftermath of the U.S.-Dakota War in Mankato, Minnesota
US Civil War: Battle of Dumfries begins in Virginia
US Navy formally commissions 1st hospital ship, the USS Red Rover - a captured Confederate sidewheel barracks ship converted for the purpose - for service on the Mississippi
James H Mason (Mass) patents 1st US coffee percolator
The 12.8-km long Fréjus Rail Tunnel through the Alps is completed.
4th largest snowfall in NYC history (18")
Socialist Labor Party of North America holds 1st national convention
1st US store to install electric lights, Philadelphia
The Harbour Grace Affray between Irish Catholics and Protestant Orangemen causes five deaths in Newfoundland.
King Mwanga of Uganda signs contract with British East Africa Company, assuring their backing as he takes the throne from his brother
Alberto Franchetti's opera "Cristoforo Colombo" opens at La Scala Teatro in Milan, Italy
Mafeking: Bathe-Powells failed assault up fort Game Tree: 24 killed
Most knock downs in a fight, Oscar Nelson (5) & Christy Williams (42)
US Government protests British interference with American merchant ships at sea, on the same day Germans announce they will treat food as contraband, subject to seizure; weakens America's protest
Joseph Joffre becomes marshal of France
1st NHL defensemen to score a goal: Toronto Maple Leaf Harry Cameron
US Federal government took over operation of American railroads for duration of WW I
1st day of 1st-class cricket in Aust after WW I (Vic v NSW)
After spending Christmas with American troops in France, Wilson goes to London for preliminary discussions about the forthcoming peace conference
1st East-West Shrine Bowl game of college football 'all-stars' played before 25,000 fans at Ewing Park, San Francisco. Proceeds continue to benefit Shriners Hospitals for Children.

NY Americans and Pittsburgh Pirates set an NHL record by recording a combined 141 shots on goal in Americans' 3-1 win; Roy Worters makes incredible 70 saves for Pittsburgh and Jake Forbes has 67 for NY
The Communist Party of India is founded
World record 10th wicket stand 307 (Kippax / Hooker) NSW v Vic
FM radio patent granted to American engineer Edwin Howard Armstrong
US forswears armed intervention in Western Hemisphere
Yomiuri Giants, Japan's 1st professional baseball team forms
Israel's Philarmonic Orchestra forms
Tom Goddard takes a cricket hat-trick for England v South Africa
Earthquake in East Anatolia Turkey
Mine strikes in Borinage Brussels
JA Fields/J Chodorov's "My Sister Eileen," premieres in NYC
British sink German battle cruiser Scharnhorst
Budapest surrounded by the Soviet army
CFP franc and CFA franc created
Flamingo Hotel in Las Vegas opens (start of an era)
British transfer Heard & McDonald Islands (Indian Ocean) to Australia
Heavy snow blankets Northeast, buries NYC under 25.8" of snow in 16 hrs That same day, LA set a record high of 84°F
Hungarian cardinal József Mindszenty arrested
"The Shadow" airs for last time on radio
RKO is 1st to announce sale of its film library to TV
The Beatles release "I Want To Hold Your Hand" and "I Saw Her Standing There" in the US
US furnishes cereal to USSR
AFL Championship, War Memorial Stadium, Buffalo: Buffalo Bills beat San Diego Chargers 20-7; defending champions can't overcome 1st quarter injury to star RB Keith Lincoln
Beatles single "I Feel Fine" goes #1 and stays #1 for 3 weeks
Moors Murderers Ian Brady and Myra Hindley claim last victim
AFL Championship, Balboa Stadium San Diego: Buffalo Bills beat San Diego Chargers 23-0; repeat previous year's win over SD
Maulana Karenga establishes Kwanzaa (1st fruits of harvest) holiday
Ballon d'Or: Ferencváros forward Flórián Albert wins award for best European football player ahead of Manchester United midfielder Bobby Charlton and Celtic winger Jimmy Johnstone
BBC television broadcasts The Beatles psychedelic travelogue "Magical Mystery Tour"
Dave Brubeck Quartet formally disbands
Arab terrorists in Athens fire on El Al plane, kills 1
Bruin Ted Green sets NHL penalty record of 3 minors, 2 majors & 2 game misconducts in a game against NY Rangers in NY's Madison Square Garden
Jay Allens "Forty Carats" premieres in NYC
Led Zeppelin's US concert debut in Denver, Colorado, as opening act for Vanilla Fudge
Soyuz 13 returns to Earth
Two Skylab 3 astronauts walk in space for a record seven hours
NHL Washington Capitals 1st franchise sellout
1st supersonic transport service (USSR-Tupolev-144)
Crowd of 85,661 attend the 1st day of the Australia vs West Indies MCG cricket test
The Communist Party of Nepal (Marxist-Leninist) is founded
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Soviet special forces take over the presidential palace in Kabul, Afghanistan
Aeroflot's Ilyushin Il-86 enters into service
One of the great day's Test Cricket at the MCG Australia v West Indies
TIME's Man of the Year is a computer
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Test Cricket debut of Gregory Richard John Matthews, v Pakistan at the MCG
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Steve Waugh makes his Test cricket debut against India at the MCG
Captured Iraqi Airways Boeing-737 in Saudi Arabia, about 60 killed
Hartford center Doug Jarvis sets NHL record of 916 consecutive games as the Whalers tie 1-1 after OT against his old club Montreal Canadiens
TV soap "Search for Tomorrow" ends 35-year run
Anti African student rebellion in Nanjing, China
Reggie Williams becomes the first general manager of the WLAF's NY-NJ Knights
Senior Professional Baseball Association folds
Militant Sikhs kill 55 and wounds 70 in India
NY Islander Derek King ties NJ Devils 5-5 with ½ second left
New York Jets announcer Marty Glickman retires at 75
The NHL's San Jose Sharks end a 13-game losing streak with a 7-2 win over the LA Kings, albeit allowing 59 shots, the most in team history; Jeff Hacket makes 57 saves
Antonov-26 crashes in Gyumri, Armenia, killing 36 people
French commandos terminate Air France hijacking in Marseille
Muttiah Muralitharan no-balled for throwing (SL v Aust, MCG)
Paul Adams becomes South Africa's youngest Test Cricket player at 18 yrs 340 days
Six-year-old beauty queen JonBenét Ramsey is found beaten and strangled in the basement of her family's home in Boulder, Colorado, seven hours after being reported as missing
Start of the largest strike in South Korean history.
The United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification goes into force
The Soufriere Hills volcano on the island of Montserrat explodes, creating a small tsunami offshore.
Iraq announces its intention to fire upon U.S. and British warplanes that patrol the northern and southern no-fly zones
Severe gales over Ireland, northern England, and southern Scotland cause widespread disruption and widespread power outages in Northern Ireland and southern Scotland
Severe weather in France kills over 100 people and causes extensive damage to property, trees and the French national power grid
Crude oil prices on the New York Mercantile Exchange (NYMEX) record one of their largest 1-day jumps of the year as traders become convinced that OPEC will follow through on production cuts
A magnitude 6.6 earthquake devastates southeast Iranian city of Bam, killing tens of thousands and destroying the citadel of Arg-é Bam.
Boxing Day shooting on a busy shopping street in Toronto kills 1 and wounds 6 others
At 40 years, 268 days, Teddy Sheringham scores for West Ham United in a 2-1 loss to Portsmouth at Upton Park, London; becomes oldest goalscorer in EPL history
The 2006 Hengchun earthquake with 7.1 magnitude hits Taiwan
China opens the world’s longest high speed rail route from Beijing to Guangzhou
Floods in Paraguay, Argentina, Brazil and Uruguay displace 150,000, blamed on El Nino
American Colin O'Brady is the first person to cross Antarctica solo and unassisted after 54 days at the Ross Ice Shelf
Annular solar eclipse visible from South and South East Asia
The Detroit Pistons suffer a 118-112 loss to the Brooklyn Nets at home to set an NBA single-season record with their 27th straight defeat
First Spanish settlement in the New World, La Navidad, is founded byChristopher Columbus(modern Môle-Saint-Nicolas in Haiti)
First known performance ofWilliam Shakespeare's tragedy "King Lear" before the court ofKing James Iat Whitehall, London
American Revolutionary War:George Washingtonleads US troops to defeat Hessians at Battle of Trenton, New Jersey
Trial of French KingLouis XVI, court hears the kings defense brought by Raymond Desèze
Decembrist uprising in Russia against TsarNicholas Ibegins [O.S. 14 December]
Gaetano Dinozetti's opera "Lucrezia Borgia" premieres at La Scala Teatro in Milan, Kingdom of Lombardy
W. S. GilbertandArthur Sullivancollaborate for the first time, on their lost opera "Thespis"; despite modest success they do not collaborate again for four years
Johannes Brahms' "Tragic Ouverture" premieres in Vienna, Austria

African-American boxerJack Johnsonstops Canadian defending championTommy Burnsin the 14th round in Sydney, Australia, to become the first black man to win world heavyweight title; Burns is favorite in 12th title defense but Johnson dominates before police stop the bout
Ambrose Bierce’s last known communication, a letter to his close friend Blanche Partington from Mexico - one of the most famous literary disappearances
Yankees and Boston Red Sox reach agreement to move future Baseball Hall of Fame pitching sluggerBabe Ruthto New York
Frances Gumm (laterJudy Garland), age 2½, billed as 'Baby Frances', makes her show business debut at her father's vaudeville theater in Grand Rapids, Minnesota
Johnny Weissmullerannounces his retirement from amateur swimming
"The Philadelphia Story" film directed byGeorge Cukor, based on the Broadway play of the same name, starringCary Grant,Katharine HepburnandJames Stewart, is released (Academy Awards Best Actor 1941)
Winston Churchillbecomes first British Prime Minister to address a joint meeting of the US Congress, warning that Axis would "stop at nothing"
Battle of Bastogne: US GeneralGeorge S. Patton's 3rd Army repulses German forces
Tennessee Williams' 1st successful play "The Glass Menagerie" premieres in Chicago
"The African Queen", directed by John Huston and based on the 1935 novel of the same name, starringHumphrey BogartandKatharine Hepburn, is released in LA, California (Academy Awards Best Actor 1952)
Swedish film "Wild Strawberries" is released, written and directed by legendary filmmakerIngmar Bergman, considered one of his best
Dmitri Shostakovich's opera "Katerina Ismailova" premieres at the Stanislavski-Nemirovich-Danchenko Musical Theatre, in Moscow, USSR
"Funny Girl" based on the life ofFanny Brice, with Barbra Streisand closes on Broadway
Paul McCartneyis interviewed on pirate radio station Radio Caroline
Jimi Hendrixwrites the song "Purple Haze" backstage at the Upper Cut Club, London
Muhammad Alifinishes off German Jürgen Blin with a thundering right cross for a 7th-round knockout in a non-title heavyweight boxing contest in Zurich, Switzerland
Ballon d'Or: Bayern Munich defenderFranz Beckenbauerwins award for best European football player ahead of tied fellow German duo ofGerd Müllerand Günter Netzer
Horror film "TheExorcist" based on book and screenplay byWilliam Peter Blatty, starring Linda Blair, rated X, premieres - 1st horror film to be nominated for Best Picture
George Harrisonteases his hit "My Sweet Lord" before breaking into "The Pirate Song" on Eric Idle's Rutland Weekend Television Christmas Special (UK)
India's former Prime Minister,Indira Gandhiis released from jail
Mark Messieris suspended for 10 games for instigating a fight with Jamie Macoun and breaking his cheekbone
Ballon d'Or: Milan's second straight 1-2-3; Dutch strikerMarco van Bastenwins his 2nd best football player in Europe title ahead of Franco Baresi and Frank Rijkaard
ODI cricket debut for Australia's Mark Taylor and Sri Lanka'sSanath Jayasuriyaat the MCG
Garry Kasparovdefeats fellow RussianAnatoly Karpovto retain the world chess championship

Future Hockey Hall of Fame right wing Joe Mullen scores 4 goals for the second straight NHL game as the Pittsburgh Penguins rout Toronto, 12-1;Mario Lemieuxadds 2 goals & 5 assists and Kevin Stevens has 2 goals & 4 assists
Future Pro Football HOF coachChuck Nollretires as head coach of the Pittsburgh Steelers (1969–1991)
Gun ofJack Ruby, killer ofLee Harvey Oswald, sells for $220,000 at auction
Spin bowling greatShane Warnebecomes first bowler in Test cricket history to take 700 wickets; bowls England opener Andrew Strauss for 50 during Australia's 4th Test win in Melbourne
Israel airstrikes on Houthi targets in Yemen narrowly misses WHO director-generalTedros Adhanom Ghebreyesusin Sanaa
A 9.3 magnitude earthquake creates a tsunami, causing devastation in Sri Lanka, India, Indonesia, Thailand, Malaysia, the Maldives, and the edges of the Indian Ocean, killing 230,000 people
Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor (1220-50), King of the Romans (Germany), Sicily and Jerusalem, born in Lesi, Italy (d. 1250)
Wilhelm Xylander, German classical scholar and humanist, born in Augsburg (d. 1576)
Yi I, Korean philosopher, writer, and Confucian scholar, born in Gangneung, Gangwon Province (d. 1584)
Elisabeth of the Palatinate, German philosopher and abbess (corresponded with René Descartes), born in Heidelberg (d. 1680)
Charles Emmanuel Biset, Flemish painter, born in Mechelen (d. between 1693–1713)
Justine Siegemund, Silesian midwife (1st woman to publish a German medical text - The Court Midwife), born in Rohnstock, Lower Silesia (d. 1705)[1]
Robert Bolling, English settler in Virginia and politician, born in London (d. 1709)
Peter Schenck, German Dutch engraver and cartographer, baptized in Elberfeld (d. 1711)
Johann Georg Pisendel, German violinist and composer, born in Cadolzburg, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1755)
Jean-François de Saint-Lambert, French poet (The Seasons, Luxury), born in Nancy, Kingdom of France (d. 1803)[1][2]
Thomas Gray, English poet (Elegy Written in a Country Church Yard), born in Cornhill, England (d. 1771)
Anthony Ziesenis, German-Dutch artist and city sculptor of Amsterdam (Gate of the Old Men's House), born in Hanover, Electorate of Hanover, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1801)[1]
Prince Josias of Saxe-Coburg-Saalfeld, German military commander in the Holy Roman Empire army, born in Coburg, Saxe-Coburg-Saafeld (d. 1815)
Thomas Nelson, American member of Continental Congress (signer of Declaration of Independence), plantation owner and Virginia militia general, born in Yorktown, Virginia (d. 1789)
Clement Mary Hofbauer, Catholic Saint, Moravian hermit and priest of the Redemptorist congregation, born in Tasovice, Margraviate of Moravia (d. 1820)
Franz Wilhelm Tausch, German clarinetist, composer, and teacher, born in Heidelberg,  Margraviate of Baden (d. 1817)
Pierre Cambronne, French general (Waterloo, Elba), born in Saint-Sébastien-sur-Loire, France (d. 1842)
Heinrich Joseph von Collin, Austria dramatist and poet (Regulus), born in Vienna (d. 1811)
Julie Clary Bonaparte, wife of Joseph Bonaparte and Queen of Naples (1806-08) and Spain and Spanish West Indies (1808-13), born in Marseille, France (d. 1845)[1]
Charles-Louis-Fleury Panckoucke, French publisher and editor (Bibliothèque latine-française), born in Paris (d. 1844)
Mary Somerville, Scottish scientist, mathematician, and first elected female Honorary Members of the Royal Astronomical Society, born in Jedburgh, Scotland (d. 1872)
Philaret Drozdov, Metropolitan of Moscow and Kolomna and influential figure in the Russian Orthodox Church (1821-67), born in Kolomna, Russian Empire (d. 1867)
Franz Hünten, German pianist and composer, born in Koblenz, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1819)
Hans Skramstad, Norwegian pianist and composer, born in Toten, Norway (d. 1839)
Joseph Léon Gatayes, French harpist and composer, born in Paris (d. 1877)
Albert Grisar, Belgian composer, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1869)
William Nelson Pendleton, American Brigadier-General (Confederate Army), born in Richmond, Virginia (d. 1883)
Wilhelm Volckmar, German organ virtuoso, composer, and teacher, born in Bad Hersfeld, Kingdom of Westphalia (d. 1887)
Israel Bush Richardson, American Major General (Union Army), born in Fairfax, Vermont (d. 1862)
E. D. E. N. [Emma Dorothy Eliza Nevitte] Southworth, American novelist (The Hidden hand, Retribution), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1899)[1][2]
Gustavus Adolphus Smith, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania (d. 1885)
Augustus Louis Chetlain, American Major General (Union Army), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1914)
Franz Coenen, Dutch violinist and composer, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1904)
Martinus Nijhoff, Dutch publisher (Van Dale) and founder (Martinus Nijhoff Publishers), born in Arnhem, Netherlands (d. 1894)
Morgan Bulkeley, American politician (US Senator - Connecticut 1905-11) and Baseball HOF administrator (NL President 1876), born in East Haddam, Connecticut (d. 1922)
Samuel "Sam" van Beem, Flemish actor (Fanfan la Tulipe), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1912)
René Bazin, French novelist of provincial life (La Terre qui Meurt / Autumn Glory; Une Tache d'Encre / A Spot of Ink), born in Angers, France (d. 1932)[1]
Arnold Mendelssohn, German composer, born in Ratibor, Province of Silesia (now Poland) (d. 1933)
Tup Scott, Australian cricket batsman and captain (8 Tests, 1 x 100, HS 102; Victoria), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1910)
Johan Frans van Bemmelen, Dutch zoologist, born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1956)
William Stephens, American politician, member of the U.S. House of Representatives, and 24th Governor of California, born in Eaton, Ohio (d. 1944)[1][2]
Friedrich Engel, German mathematician (group theory), born in Lugau, Saxony, German Federation (d. 1941)
Alexander Amfiteatrov, Russian-Italian journalist, newspaper editor, novelist, and historian, born in Kaluga, Russia (d. 1923)
Harry Redman, American composer, writer, and artist, born in Illinois (d. 1958)
Norman Angell, British lecturer, journalist, author (The Great Illusion), politician and 1933 Nobel Peace Prize laureate, born in Holbeach, England (d. 1967)
Hubert Cuypers, Dutch organist, composer, and choir conductor (Minstrels), born in Baexem, Netherlands (d. 1960)
Karel Moor, Czech composer and conductor, born in Belgrade, Kingdom of Bohemia, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1945)
Isaiah Bowman, American geographer (co-founder of the Geographical Review), born in Waterloo, Ontario (d. 1950)
Armen Tigran Tigranyan, Armenian music composer and conductor, born in Alexandropol, Armenia (d. 1950)
Julius Weismann, German pianist, conductor, and composer (Schwanenweiß), born in Freiburg im Breisgau, German Empire (d. 1950)
Carl Ahues, German Chess Master (1950), born in Bremen, Germany (d. 1968)
Frank Debenham, Australian geographer and geologist, born in Bowral, Australia (d. 1965)
Félix Éboué, French colonial administrator, born in Cayenne, French Guiana (d. 1944)
Arthur Percival, British Army officer in World War I and II, born in Aspenden, England (d. 1966)
Uncle Charlie Osborne [Charles Osborne], American fiddler (Relics and Treasure, One Hundred Years Farther On), born in Russell County, Virginia (d. 1992)[1]
Evgeni Tikotsky, Polish-Belorussian composer, born in St. Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1970)
Yury Nikolayevich Tyulin, Russian composer and music theorist, born in Reval, Russian Empire (d. 1976)
Antonio Molina, Filipino composer, conductor and music administrator (Ana Maria, Hatinggabi), born in Quiapo, Manila, Captaincy General of the Philippines (d. 1980)
Jean Toomer, American poet and novelist (Cane, Blue Meridian), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1967)[1]
Willy Corsari, Dutch writer (the Dutch Agatha Christie) and actor, born in Jette, Belgium (d. 1998)
Victor Hely-Hutchinson, British composer (Carol Symphony), born in Cape Town, Cape Colony, Africa (d. 1947)
Anatoli Lvovich Kaplan, Russian draftsman and lithographer (By the River, The Bewitched Tailor), born in Rogachov, Russian Empire (d. 1980)[1][2]
Elisha Cook Jr, American actor (Maltese Falcon, Shane, Magnum PI), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1995)
Alenjo Carpentier, Cuban-French writer (Guerra del Tiempo), born in Lausanne, Switzerland (d. 1980)
Young Stribling, American boxer (vs Max Schmeling, Ring Magazine fight of the year 1931), born in Bainbridge, Georgia (d. 1933)
Ralph Hill, American athlete (Olympic silver 5,000m 1932; same time as winner Lauri Lehtinen), born in Klamath Falls, Oregon (d. 1994)
Imperio Argentina, Argentine actress and singer, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 2003)
Lucy Faithfull, British children's campaigner, born in South Africa (d. 1996)
Marguerite Churchill, American stage and screen actress (Dracula's Daughter; The Big Trail), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 2000)
Renato Guttuso, Italian painter (Flight From Etna; Crucifixion), theater set designer, and politician, born in Bagheria, Sicily, Italy (d. 1987)
Steve Kordek, American pinball machine designer, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2012)
Arsenio Lacson, Filipino journalist and politician (Mayor of Manila, 1952-62), born in Talisay, Philippines (d. 1962)
Gordon Onslow Ford, English surrealist painter, born in Wendover, England (d. 2003)
Frank Swift, English soccer goalkeeper and journalist (19 caps; Manchester City 376 games; died Munich air disaster), born in Blackpool, Lancashire (d. 1958)
Annemarie Wendl, German actress (Lindenstraße, Der Seitensprung), born in Trostberg, Germany (d. 2006)
Richard Widmark, American actor (Madigan, Murder on the Orient Express, Judgement at Nuremberg), born in Sunrise Township, Minnesota (d. 2008)
Gerda Neumann, Danish film actress, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1947)
H. A. Gomperts, Dutch writer (Duck on Attic), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1998)
Una Mae Carlisle, American jazz pianist and singer ("Walkin' By The River"), born in Xenia, Ohio; (d. 1956)
Helmut Eder, Austrian composer, born in Linz, Austria (d. 2005)
Rose Mary Woods, American secretary to Richard Nixon (claimed to have erased part of Watergate tapes by accident), born in Sebring, Ohio (d. 2005)
Georgios Rallis, Greek conservative politician and Prime Minister of Greece (1980-81), born in Athens, Greece (d. 2006)[1]
Gote Carlid, Swedish composer, born in Hogbo near Sandviken, Sweden (d. 1953)
John Severin, American comic book artist (Hulk, Mad Magazine), born in Jersey City, New Jersey (d. 2012)
Iain Watson Gallaway, New Zealand sports broadcaster (Radio Sport; Halberg Award) and author ("Not a Cloud in the Sky"), born in Dunedin, New Zealand (d. 2021)
Norman Orentreich, American dermatologist known as "the father of modern hair transplantation", born in New York City (d. 2019)
Richard Mayes, British actor (Doctor Who, Emmerdale), born in Luton, Bedfordshire, England (d. 2006)
Frank Broyles, American collegiate athlete, and administrator (University of Arkansas Athletic Director, 1974-2007), born in Decatur, Georgia (d. 2017)
Glenn Davis, American College Football Hall of Fame halfback (Heisman Trophy 1946; National C'ship 1944, 45, 46; Army; NFL: LA Rams), born in Claremont, California (d. 2005)
Baron Baker, Jamaican-born British activist, born in Port Antonia, Jamaica (d. 1996)
Ali Javan, Iranian American physicist and inventor (Helium-Neon Laser), born in Tehran, Iran (d. 2016)[1]
Earle Brown, American composer (open form), born in Lunenburg, Massachusetts (d. 2002)
Edgar Ngoyi, South African anti-apartheid activist and ANC leader who spent 17 years in Robben Island Prison, born in Eastern Cape, Union of South Africa (d. 2007)
Zlatko Pibernik, Croatian composer and music teacher, born in Zagreb, Yugoslavia (d. 2010)
Alan King [Irwin Kniberg], American comedian and actor (Anderson Tapes; Memories of Me), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2004)
Bill Yeoman, American College Football Hall of Fame coach (University of Houston 1962-86; record 160–108–8), born in Elnora, Indiana (d. 2020)
Denis Quilley, British actor (Anything Goes; The Lady's Not For Burning; Masada), born in London, England (d. 2003)
Stu Miller, American MLB baseball pitcher, 1952-68 (St. Louis Cardinals; New York/San Francisco Giants, and 3 other teams), born in Northampton, Massachusetts (d. 2015)
Régine [Zylberberg], French singer and nightclub owner, born in Anderlecht, Belgium (d. 2022)
Donald Moffat, British actor (Kent-Logan's Run), born in Plymouth, Devon (d. 2018)
Jean Ferrat, French singer-songwriter, born in Vaucresson, France (d. 2010)
Sayed Mutawli imam ad-Darsh, Egyptian-British scholar, Muslim leader, and broadcaster, born in Kafila, Egypt (d. 1997)
Thomas Eden Binkley, American lutenist and early music scholar, born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1995)
Don Furner, Australian rugby league forward (1 Test) and coach (Australia 15 Tests; Eastern Suburbs, Canberra Raiders), born in Condobolin, Australia (d. 2020)
Walter Leblanc, Belgian avant-garde painter and visual artist, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1986)
Billy Bean, American jazz guitarist (John Lewis; Herbie Mann; Bud Shank), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2012)
Caroll Spinney, American puppeteer behind Big Bird and Oscar the Grouch on Sesame Street (1969-2018), born in Waltham, Massachusetts (d. 2019)
Barbara Jacket, American athletics coach (Head coach US women's team Olympics 1992; International Women's Sports HOF), born in Port Arthur, Texas (d. 2022)
Martín Pando, Argentine soccer forward (11 caps; Argentinos Juniors, River Plate), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 2021)
Abdul "Duke" Fakir, American rock vocalist (Four Tops), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2024)[1]
Noel Kelehan, Irish pianist, conductor (RTÉ Concert Orchestra), and arranger (Eurovision; U2), born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 2012)
Rale Rasic, Australian soccer coach (Australia 1969-74; Marconi Stallions FC) and broadcaster (SBS), born in Dole, Bosnia and Herzegovina (d. 2023)
Rohan Kanhai, West Indian cricket batsmen (79 Tests, 15 x 100, HS 256; Guyana, Warwickshire CCC), born in Port Mourant, British Guiana
Kitty Dukakis, American activist known for speaking openly about her history of depression and substance abuse, born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 2025)[1]
Vitaliy Hodziatsky, Ukrainian composer and educator, born in Kiev, Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic
Jay Heimowitz, American poker player (six bracelets), born in Bethel, New York[1][2]
John Horton Conway, British mathematician (cellular automaton Game of Life), born in Liverpool, England (d. 2020)
Fred Schepisi, Australian director (A Cry in the Dark, Empire Falls), born in Melbourne, Australia
Lynn Martin, American politician, (Secretary of Labor, 1991-93; US Congress, 1981-91), born in Evanston, Illinois
Edward C. Prescott, American economist and winner of the Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences 2004, born in Glens Falls, New York (d. 2022)[1]
Ray Sadecki, American MLB player (St. Louis Cardinals), born in Kansas City, KS, (d. 2014)
Klaus Bonsack, German luger (Olympic gold GDR men's doubles 1968, bronze 1972; silver singles 1964, bronze 1968; World C'ship gold 1967), born in Waltershausen, Germany (d. 2023)
Alain Plantefol, French rugby union second rower (11 Tests; Racing CF, SU Agen), born in Colombes, France (d. 2022)
Antonio Juliano, Italian soccer midfielder (13 caps; Napoli 394 games, Bologna), born in Naples, Italy (d. 2023)
Barry Wood, English cricket batsman (12 Tests, 2 x 50; Lancashire CCC, Derbyshire CCC), born in Ossett, England
